skill.name: metagenomic-krona-chart
skill.description: Analyze data with metagenomic-krona-chart using a reproducible workflow, explicit validation, and structured outputs for review-ready interpretation.
skill.summary: Generate interactive Krona sunburst charts showing taxonomic abundance from Kraken2, Bracken, and custom TSV metagenomic data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: After evaluating 124 static findings across 8 files (1120 lines), all detections are false positives. The skill is a legitimate bioinformatics tool that parses metagenomic classification data and generates interactive HTML charts. The weak cryptographic algorithm detections are Python dictionary operations misidentified by the scanner. System reconnaissance detections are standard file I/O operations. Shell command detections are documentation examples in markdown code blocks showing legitimate usage. Path traversal detections are from a security checklist, not executable code. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or suspicious behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detections - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 21 dictionary/map operations in scripts/main.py as weak cryptographic algorithms. The code performs no cryptographic operations. Lines flagged include standard Python dictionary assignments (nodes[tax_id] = node), lookups, and DataFrame operations. These are legitimate taxonomic node storage operations for a metagenomic data visualization tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual code review confirms zero cryptographic operations in scripts/main.py. All flagged lines are dictionary/hash map operations for storing and retrieving taxonomy nodes.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 31 standard file I/O and system operations as system reconnaissance. The code uses open(), sys.argv, Path(), sys.exit(), and argparse for legitimate purposes: reading input files, validating file existence, parsing command-line arguments, and managing error states. This is standard behavior for a command-line data processing tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged operations (sys.argv, open(), Path.exists()) are standard Python I/O patterns for command-line tools. No evidence of directory enumeration, user discovery, or system profiling.

low_findings.title: External Commands in Markdown Documentation - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 54 shell command examples in markdown files as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These commands appear inside markdown code blocks (```bash ... ```) in SKILL.md, README.md, and references/runtime_checklist.md. They are documentation examples showing users how to run python scripts/main.py, pip install, and py_compile. No backtick execution occurs in executable Python code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual verification confirms all flagged external commands are inside markdown fenced code blocks serving as user documentation. No os.system(), subprocess, or backtick execution exists in the Python source code.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequences in Security Checklist - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged ../ patterns at SKILL.md lines 179 and 182. These strings appear inside a risk assessment security checklist table documenting validation rules: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These are documentation describing security measures, not executable path traversal code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged ../ patterns are inside a markdown table as documentation for input validation rules. The actual Python code in scripts/main.py validates input file paths using Path.exists() with no path traversal risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in Source Attribution - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged a URL at SKILL.md line 7. This URL is a source attribution link pointing to the GitHub repository (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). It is documentation metadata, not a network call in executable code. The skill makes no network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is documentation-only metadata inside a markdown blockquote. No HTTP requests, fetch calls, or network connections exist in scripts/main.py.

low_findings.title: Memory-Mapped File Access in Sample Data - False Positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged data files example/sample_custom.tsv and example/sample_kraken2.txt as memory-mapped file access. These are sample taxonomic abundance data files used for testing the chart generator. They contain tab-separated taxonomy hierarchy data (taxon IDs, names, ranks, read counts). No memory mapping is performed on these files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are static data files containing taxonomic abundance values. The Python code reads them using standard open() and pd.read_csv(), not memory mapping.

content.user_title: Create Interactive Krona Charts for Metagenomic Data
content.value_statement: Metagenomic researchers need clear visual summaries of complex taxonomic abundance data. This skill generates interactive Krona sunburst charts from Kraken2, Bracken, and custom TSV files with automatic format detection and configurable visualization settings.
actual_capabilities: Parse Kraken2 and Bracken report formats into taxonomic hierarchy trees
Parse custom TSV files with flexible column mapping for taxonomic data
Auto-detect input file format to reduce manual configuration
Generate interactive HTML sunburst charts with zoom and hover details
Filter low-abundance taxa using configurable minimum percentage threshold
Color-code taxonomic ranks (domain through species) for visual clarity
limitations: Cannot perform taxonomic classification on raw sequencing data
Input files must already contain pre-computed classification with taxonomic hierarchy
Very large datasets with thousands of taxa may produce large HTML output files
Does not compare multiple samples in a single chart view

use_cases.title: Visualize Microbiome Sample Composition
use_cases.description: A bench scientist runs Kraken2 on a 16S sequencing sample and uses this skill to generate an interactive sunburst chart showing bacterial phyla, genera, and species abundance for a research presentation.

use_cases.title: Integrate Chart Generation into Automated Pipelines
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics engineer incorporates this skill into a Nextflow or Snakemake pipeline to automatically generate Krona charts for each sample after taxonomic classification.

use_cases.title: Document Clinical Metagenomic Findings
use_cases.description: A clinical microbiologist loads a custom-formatted TSV file with curated taxonomic abundance data and generates a publication-ready interactive chart for a clinical study report.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Krona Chart from Kraken2 Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Krona chart from my Kraken2 report at [input_file_path]. Save the chart as [output_filename].html.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with a standard Kraken2 classification report who wants a quick visual summary.

prompt_templates.title: Customized Chart with Filter Thresholds
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Krona chart from [input_file] with minimum display threshold of [percent] percent and maximum depth of [depth] levels. Title it '[chart_title]' and save as [output_filename].html.
prompt_templates.scenario: User who needs to focus on high-abundance taxa and limit chart complexity.

prompt_templates.title: Parse Custom TSV Format
prompt_templates.prompt: My custom TSV file at [filepath] has taxonomic hierarchy with columns taxon_id, name, rank, parent_id, reads, and percent. Generate a Krona chart using the custom format parser. Set type to custom explicitly.
prompt_templates.scenario: User with a non-standard taxonomy file format who needs explicit format selection.

prompt_templates.title: Full Workflow with Validation and Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the complete metagenomic Krona chart workflow on [input_file]: validate the file exists, auto-detect its format, parse the taxonomy tree, generate an interactive chart with optimal settings based on data size, and provide a summary of the top taxonomic groups by abundance.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user who wants a reproducible workflow with validation steps and output interpretation.

output_examples.input: A Kraken2 report file with 990,000 classified reads across 3 domains, 5 phyla, and 12 genera from a gut microbiome sample.
output_examples.output: An interactive HTML sunburst chart showing taxonomic hierarchy from domain to species level. The outer rings display individual genera with color-coded segments based on rank. Hovering over a segment shows the taxon name, rank, read count, and percentage. The center displays total reads. A legend on the right maps rank colors.

output_examples.input: A custom TSV file with 25 taxonomy nodes spanning root, domain, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species ranks with associated read counts and percentages.
output_examples.output: A responsive HTML sunburst chart with Bacteria (95%), Eukaryota (4%), Viruses (2%), and Archaea (1%) as the main segments. Each segment is color-coded by taxonomic rank. Users can click to zoom into specific branches and hover for detailed abundance information.

best_practices: Validate that input files contain pre-computed taxonomic classifications before running the chart generator.
Set a minimum percentage threshold of 0.1 or higher for large datasets to keep charts readable and focused on abundant taxa.
Review the generated HTML chart to verify taxonomic hierarchy is correctly parsed before sharing results.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to classify raw sequencing reads - it only visualizes pre-computed taxonomic assignments.
Do not manually edit or reorder rows in input TSV files, as this can break parent-child hierarchy relationships.
Do not skip input file validation - always confirm the file exists and has the expected format before generating charts.

faq.question: What input formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Kraken2 reports, Bracken reports, and custom TSV files with columns for taxon_id, name, rank, parent_id, reads, and percent. The format can be auto-detected or specified manually.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with raw FASTQ sequencing data?
faq.answer: No. This skill only visualizes data that has already been classified by tools like Kraken2 or Bracken. It does not perform taxonomic classification.

faq.question: How do I filter out low-abundance taxa?
faq.answer: Use the min-percent parameter to set a minimum abundance threshold. For example, setting 0.1 will hide taxa with less than 0.1 percent abundance.

faq.question: Where is the HTML chart saved?
faq.answer: The chart is saved to the path specified with the output parameter. The default filename is krona_chart.html in the current working directory.

faq.question: What Python libraries are needed?
faq.answer: The script requires Python 3.8 or later with plotly (5.0+) and pandas (1.3+). Install them using: pip install plotly pandas.

faq.question: Can I view the chart on any computer?
faq.answer: Yes. The output is a standalone HTML file that can be opened in any modern web browser without additional software or internet access.
